text: Thomas Jefferson famously claimed that all men are created equal. In practice, this has never been true for migrants. In this new collection, twelve leading migration scholars show how states around the world have distinguished and legislated different categories of migrants (in France, the United States, Turkey, Canada, Mexico, the Netherlands, Sweden and Denmark, among other places). This book is constructed as a triptych with each panel corresponding to one of the three central questions in the migration field: who is a refugee? Who is a family? And what is difference? All three categories and their interconnections are explored through detailed ethnographic analyses that highlight the gender, class, ethnic, religious and sexuality dynamics at play, which are shaped by political economy and structural factors as much as by everyday social and cultural interactions.
